danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy sprite appearance in overworld and dungeons
danger_signals.meaning: Active threats to humanity; indicates areas of conflict and danger

danger_signals.visual_form: Large boss sprite (Maldra the Dragon), distinctive visual scale
danger_signals.meaning: Major world threat; marks narrative turning point and end of first act

danger_signals.visual_form: Monster behavior and placement in human settlements and dungeons
danger_signals.meaning: Systemic threat requiring player intervention; world-state of conflict

safety_signals.visual_form: Pet dog (Mac/Johnny) and other animal sprites equipped alongside Corona
safety_signals.meaning: Ally presence; Corona gains ability to communicate with and recruit animals for protection

safety_signals.visual_form: Human settlement environment, safe zone aesthetic
safety_signals.meaning: Human civilization hub; place of law and structure where Corona begins his journey

safety_signals.visual_form: Visual feedback when animals are active (e.g., penguin ice effects on sword)
safety_signals.meaning: Active protection and power increase; enhanced combat capability

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Sword and shield inherited from Corona's father
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Soleil Town; human settlements requiring defense

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Diverse enemy sprites representing different monster types
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Dungeons and monster-occupied areas; threat zones outside human settlements
Found throughout overworld; can be recruited as companions

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Monster presence in overworld and dungeons; Soleil Town as defended settlement
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Corona cannot speak to humans, only to animals; monsters actively threaten civilization

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Time-travel mechanic visually represented through environmental changes
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Corona regains ability to speak to humans; story shifts to exploring historical context and world-building

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Environmental changes through time travel; visual narrative of conflict origin
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player gains contextual understanding through NPC dialogue and environmental storytelling

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dialogue system restriction; animal-focused conversation UI
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Gameplay constraint that forces player into animal-companion recruitment mechanic

environmental_storytelling.example: Soleil Town as safe human hub contrasts with monster-infested dungeons and overworld danger zones
Corona's sword and shield from deceased father establish narrative weight and connect player to world history
Animal abilities (ice penguin, holding dog) unlock new areas and passages, implying environmental puzzle design tied to companion recruitment
Corona's inability to speak to humans forces reliance on animal communication, visually reinforcing his isolation and the protagonist's abnormal status
Visual changes in Act 2 reveal historical context of human-monster conflict through environmental transformation

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Cannot speak to humans; can only communicate with animals; actively recruiting companions for combat support
Regains human speech capability; transitions from exploration/recruitment to conflict-understanding; can now interact with human NPCs
Found in overworld; recruitabile; communicate through dialogue; waiting for Corona to engage
Follow Corona; provide special combat abilities (e.g., penguin ice attacks); enable passage through previously blocked areas
Actively threaten human settlements and overworld; occupy dungeons; represent ongoing conflict
Marks narrative checkpoint; defeat triggers Act 2 transition and world-state shift

notes: Crusader of Centy communicates world-state primarily through dialogue restrictions (Corona's speech loss) and narrative progression gates. The core visual manifestation of danger vs. safety relies on contrasting environments (settlements vs. monster zones) and enemy sprite design. Factions are implicit rather than visually distinct—humans are represented through location (Soleil Town), monsters through enemy design, and animals through companion recruitment. The game uses ability-gating (animal companions unlocking new areas) as a visual reward system that reinforces world exploration and environmental narrative. The time-travel mechanic in Act 2 appears to be the primary visual method for communicating historical context and conflict causality. No explicit color-coding or sigil systems are documented for faction identification.
